one_line_takeaway: State-of-the-art LLM membership-inference attacks collapse toward random-guessing AUC when the accused paraphrases training text with SAGE (an SAE-guided, model-internals-free rewriting pipeline that preserves meaning and utility while destroying lexical overlap), showing MIAs rely on surface-level artifacts and are evidentiarily too brittle to stand alone as proof of copyright infringement in adversarial litigation settings.
key_finding: Under LoRA fine-tuning (Table 1, AUC averaged across the 9 query-based attacks, excluding Bag-of-Words control), MIA AUC drops from FT to SAGE to SAGE-R consistently across all five datasets: arXiv 0.68 to 0.60 to 0.55; Wikipedia 0.68 to 0.60 to 0.53; Pile-CC 0.67 to 0.56 to 0.52; HackerNews 0.69 to 0.58 to 0.53; PubMed 0.66 to 0.57 to 0.54 -- SAGE-R pushes AUC close to the pretrained (~random, AUC~0.5) baseline. TPR@1%FPR (Table 2) shows an even sharper collapse, e.g., the strongest attack (reference-based Ratio) drops from 0.200/0.164/0.185/0.122/0.190 (FT, per dataset) to 0.077/0.073/0.034/0.032/0.072 (SAGE) to 0.013/0.020/0.015/0.016/0.058 (SAGE-R). The qualitative ordering FT > SOFT > SAGE > SAGE-R > PT holds in both LoRA and full fine-tuning regimes (Figure 3), with full fine-tuning producing uniformly higher (harder-to-eliminate) leakage than LoRA. Notably, SAGE achieves stronger MIA suppression than SOFT despite SOFT requiring access to the target model's internals for its selective obfuscation, while SAGE is model-internals-free. Utility (LLM-as-a-Judge, Table 3, averaged across datasets) is preserved or improved under SAGE relative to FT (Average: PT 47.8, FT 49.5, SAGE 52.8) and roughly maintained under SAGE-R (49.1), i.e., paraphrasing that defeats MIAs does not measurably erode downstream knowledge retention. The authors explicitly flag as a limitation that under full fine-tuning, MIA AUC remains substantially above chance (0.7-0.8) even after paraphrasing, so some memorization signal survives -- the core claim is about evidentiary robustness/threshold stability for legal admissibility, not that MIAs become fully useless.
core_contribution: The paper (1) formalizes copyright auditing of LLM training data as a judge-prosecutor-accused communication protocol, defining Semantic Equivalence (Def. 2.1) and a formal Robustness criterion (Def. 2.2) that any admissible membership-inference-based evidence must satisfy under semantics-preserving transformations available to an accused party; (2) introduces SAGE (Structure-Aware SAE-Guided Extraction), a paraphrasing pipeline that decomposes a document into structural sections (headers, citations, identifiers, code) preserved verbatim and narrative sections that are rewritten by an LLM under a joint objective maximizing Semantic Persistence Score (SPS, computed from Sparse Autoencoder feature-activation cosine similarity on narrative spans) while minimizing WordSim (an average of word-Jaccard, word-trigram overlap, and character-5-gram overlap) -- SAGE operates without access to the audited model's internals, using an external, independent SAE oracle (trained on gemma-2b, layer 12); it also defines SAGE-R, a variant that additionally strips document structure and masks LLM-tagged factual anchors (names, numbers, dates; ~9.8% of tokens on average); and (3) empirically shows, via systematic MIA evaluation, that state-of-the-art membership inference attacks degrade substantially against models fine-tuned on SAGE/SAGE-R paraphrases even though the model's downstream knowledge/utility is preserved -- concluding that current MIAs are evidentiarily brittle and insufficient as a standalone technical basis for copyright auditing of LLM training data.
threat_model: Three-party adversarial protocol: a Prosecutor supplies a suspect text x and black-box query access to a deployed model f_theta and claims x was used in training; an Accused (model owner/developer) wants to minimize legal/financial exposure and may legally or strategically apply semantics-preserving transformations (paraphrasing, summarization, stylistic rewriting) to training data before fine-tuning; a Judge must decide membership by thresholding an MIA score s(x) at tau_mia without ever observing the original training corpus. The paper's threat model is specifically an anti-auditing/evidence-evasion setting -- the 'attacker' from the auditing perspective is the accused model developer obfuscating data, and the object under test is whether MIA-derived evidence remains valid (Definition 2.2, robustness to a transformation family T with epsilon_rob bound) under that adversarial obfuscation. Access model for the auditor (prosecutor) is explicitly black-box: query-only access to outputs, token probabilities, or likelihoods, not internal states. SAGE/SAGE-R themselves require no access to the target model's internals, only to a separate, fixed semantic-oracle SAE and paraphrasing LLMs.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: paraphrasing does not fully eliminate memorization -- MIAs retain AUC 0.7-0.8 under full fine-tuning, well above chance; the paper argues aggregate statistical signal above chance does not equal a stable, admissible decision threshold under Definition 2.2, since semantics-preserving transformations shift score distributions enough to destabilize any fixed tau_mia. Findings are scoped to the text modality only; the authors explicitly note other modalities (image, code generation) may show different trade-offs. The reproduced SOFT baseline diverges from originally reported numbers, requiring a corrected implementation (discussed in Appendix C), which the authors flag as a discrepancy with prior published results. SAGE's factual-anchor removal in SAGE-R depends on an LLM-prompting tagger for entities/numbers/dates, an imperfect and unverified extraction step. The evaluation excludes adaptive/self-calibrating attacks such as SPV-MIA (deliberately, since the authors treat dynamic calibration as raising distinct robustness/admissibility questions left to future work). The paper does not propose a legal standard -- it explicitly frames Definition 2.2 as a technical formalization only, not a claim about actual admissibility doctrine.

datasets_used: MIMIR benchmark (Duan et al., 2024), 13-gram (13, 0.8) split -- arXiv subset
quantitative_results: LoRA fine-tuning average MIA AUC (excluding Bag-of-Words control), FT to SAGE to SAGE-R: arXiv 0.68/0.60/0.55; Wikipedia 0.68/0.60/0.53; Pile-CC 0.67/0.56/0.52; HackerNews 0.69/0.58/0.53; PubMed 0.66/0.57/0.54
LoRA fine-tuning average TPR@1%FPR, FT to SAGE to SAGE-R: arXiv 0.06/0.03/0.01; Wikipedia 0.06/0.03/0.02; Pile-CC 0.05/0.02/0.01; HackerNews 0.04/0.02/0.01; PubMed 0.06/0.02/0.02

Reference-based Ratio attack TPR@1%FPR (strongest attack), FT to SAGE to SAGE-R: arXiv 0.200/0.077/0.013; Wikipedia 0.164/0.073/0.020; Pile-CC 0.185/0.034/0.015; HackerNews 0.122/0.032/0.016; PubMed 0.190/0.072/0.058
Full fine-tuning: MIA AUC remains 0.7-0.8 even after SAGE/SAGE-R paraphrasing (stated as a limitation, per-dataset breakdown in Appendix H)

LLM-as-a-Judge utility scores (averaged over 3 paraphrasers, LoRA setting), PT/FT/SAGE/SAGE-R: ArXiv 45.7/47.0/53.1/52.8; Wikipedia 46.6/48.3/54.6/49.0; Pile-CC 45.1/51.6/49.4/45.2; PubMed 53.9/55.9/60.9/60.0; HackerNews 42.1/45.0/45.9/39.3; Average 47.8/49.5/52.8/49.1
SAGE-R removes approximately 9.8% of tokens on average (factual anchors)
Pretrained (PT) model MIA AUC is approximately 0.5 (random guessing) across all five datasets, used as sanity check
Qualitative ordering of MIA leakage holds across both fine-tuning regimes: FT > SOFT > SAGE > SAGE-R > PT
baselines_compared: 9 MIAs: Loss (Yeom et al., 2018), Zlib (Carlini et al., 2021), Lowercase (Carlini et al., 2021), Min-K% Prob (Shi et al., 2024), Min-K%++ (Zhang et al., 2024), ReCall (Xie et al., 2024), CON-ReCall (Wang et al., 2025), Bag-of-Words (Meeus et al., 2025; Das et al., 2025, no-model-access lexical control excluded from averages), Ratio (Carlini et al., 2021, reference-based, using OpenLLaMA-7B shadow model)
SOFT (Zhang et al., 2025) -- reproduced/corrected selective-obfuscation defense baseline requiring access to model internals
Training/defense regimes compared: Pretrained (PT), standard Fine-tuned (FT), SOFT, SAGE, SAGE-R
